extra.message: User input from request.form['expression'] (line 116) is passed directly to eval() on line 120, allowing arbitrary Python code execution on the server.

User input from request.form['address'] (line 136) is concatenated into a shell command passed to rp() (intended to be popen from os module imported on line 4). This allows arbitrary OS command execution.

XML parser is configured with no_network=False and dtd_validation=True, allowing XML External Entity (XXE) attacks. User-supplied XML from request.form['xml'] (line 156) is parsed with this insecure parser on line 159, enabling file reads, SSRF, and denial of service.

User input from request.form['name'] (line 189) is interpolated into a template string via Python %s formatting on lines 189 and 204, then rendered with render_template_string() on line 205. This allows Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) where an attacker can inject Jinja2 expressions like {{ config }} to access application internals or achieve remote code execution.
The parsed_xml variable derived from user-supplied XML input (line 156) is rendered directly into HTML output without any sanitization or escaping, allowing reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

User input from request.form['name'] is injected into HTML via Python string formatting on line 189 before being rendered with render_template_string() on line 205. Since the input is concatenated directly (not via Jinja2 {{ }} variable substitution), auto-escaping is bypassed, allowing reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).
The output of eval(expression) from user input (line 116) is rendered directly into the HTML response body without sanitization or escaping, allowing reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).
The output of the shell command from user-supplied address (line 136) is rendered directly into the HTML response body without sanitization or escaping, allowing reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).

The Flask secret_key 'Harry Potter And The Deathly Hallows' is hardcoded in the source code. Anyone with access to this key can forge session cookies, bypassing authentication and tampering with session data. Additionally, if the code is committed to version control, the key is permanently exposed.

Flask debug mode is enabled (app.debug = True). The Werkzeug debugger exposes an interactive Python console that can be used for remote code execution. It also discloses detailed stack traces and application source code to users.

User passwords are stored and compared in cleartext (password=passw filter on line 50, and stored via User model on lines 30 and 62 without any hashing). This means a database compromise exposes all user passwords in plaintext, violating secure credential storage best practices.

cwe: CWE-312: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information

extra.message: The raw error object is passed to the error.html template on line 109 and rendered via {{ error }} (template error.html line 20). This can leak internal stack traces, file paths, database schema information, and other sensitive application details to end users.

cwe: CWE-209: Information Exposure Through Error Messages

extra.message: All state-changing POST endpoints (/login, /register/, /evaluate, /lookup, /xml, /sayhi) lack CSRF protection. No CSRF tokens are embedded in forms or validated on the server, allowing cross-site request forgery attacks that could force authenticated users to perform unintended actions.
